verses.text: Its length (62 pages folio), the physical weakness of the speaker, and the limits of the endurance of even an interested audience, incline us to suppose that it was not spoken in its present form.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We cannot well set aside expressions which clearly point to actual delivery, but it may have been amplified later.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It has then been ordained that the great Basil, who used so constantly to furnish me with subjects for my discourses, of which he was quite as proud as any other man of his own, should himself now furnish me with the grandest subject which has ever fallen to the lot of an orator.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For I think that if anyone desired, in making trial of his powers of eloquence, to test them by the standard of that one of all his subjects which he preferred (as painters do with epoch-making pictures), he would choose that which stood first of all others, but would set aside this as beyond the powers of human eloquence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So great a task is the praise of such a man, not only to me, who have long ago laid aside all thought of emulation, but even to those who live for eloquence, and whose sole object is the gaining of glory by subjects like this.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such is my opinion, and, as I persuade myself, with perfect justice.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But I know not what subject I can treat with eloquence, if not this; or what greater favour I can do to myself, to the admirers of virtue, or to eloquence itself, than express our admiration for this man.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: To me it is the discharge of a most sacred debt.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And our speech is a debt beyond all others due to those who have been gifted, in particular, with powers of speech.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: To the admirers of virtue a discourse is at once a pleasure and an incentive to virtue.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For when I have learned the praises of men, I have a distinct idea of their progress: now, there is none of us all, within whose power it is not to attain to any point whatsoever in that progress.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: As for eloquence itself, in either case, all must go well with it.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For, if the discourse be almost worthy of its subject—eloquence will have given an exhibition of its power: if it fall far short of it, as must be the case when the praises of Basil are being set forth, by an actual demonstration of its incapacity, it will have declared the superiority of the excellences of its subject to all expression in words.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: These are the reasons which have urged me to speak, and to address myself to this contest.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And at my late appearance, long after his praises have been set forth by so many, who have publicly and privately done him honour, let no one be surprised.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Yea, may I be pardoned by that divine soul, the object of my constant reverence!

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And as, when he was amongst us, he constantly corrected me in many points, according to the rights of a friend and the still higher law; for I am not ashamed to say this, for he was a standard of virtue to us all; so now, looking down upon me from above, he will treat me with indulgence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I ask pardon too of any here who are among his warmest admirers, if indeed anyone can be warmer than another, and we are not all abreast in our zeal for his good fame.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For it is not contempt which has caused me to fall short of what might have been expected of me: nor have I been so regardless of the claims of virtue or of friendship; nor have I thought that to praise him befitted any other more than me.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: No! my first reason was, that I shrunk from this task, for I will say the truth, as priests do, who approach their sacred duties before being cleansed both in voice and mind.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In the second place, I remind you, though you know it well, of the task in which I was engaged on behalf of the true doctrine, which had been properly forced upon me, and had carried me from home, according, as I suppose, to the will of God, and certainly according to the judgment of our noble champion of the truth, the breath of whose life was pious doctrine alone, such as promotes the salvation of the whole world.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: As for my bodily health, I ought not, perhaps, to dare to mention it, when my subject is a man so doughty in his conquest of the body, even before his removal hence, and who maintained that no powers of the soul should suffer hindrance from this our fetter.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So much for my defence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I do not think I need labour it further, in speaking of him to you who know so clearly my affairs.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I must now proceed with my eulogy, commending myself to his God, in order that my commendations may not prove an insult to the man, and that I may not lag far behind all others; even though we all equally fall as far short of his due, as those who look upon the heavens or the rays of the Sun.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Had I seen him to be proud of his birth, and the rights of birth, or any of those infinitely little objects of those whose eyes are on the ground, we should have had to inspect a new catalogue of the Heroes.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What details as to his ancestors might I not have laid under contribution!

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Nor would even history have had any advantage over me, since I claim this advantage, that his celebrity depends, not upon fiction or legend, but upon actual facts attested by many witnesses.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: On his father’s side Pontus offers to me many details, in no wise inferior to its wonders of old time, of which all history and poesy are full; there are many others concerned with this my native land, of illustrious men of Cappadocia, renowned for its youthful progeny, no less than for its horses.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Accordingly we match with his father’s family that of his mother.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What family owns more numerous, or more illustrious generals and governors, or court officials, or again, men of wealth, and lofty thrones, and public honours, and oratorical renown?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If it were permitted me to wish to mention them, I would make nothing of the Pelopidæ and Cecropidæ, the Alcmæonids, the Æacidæ, and Heracleidæ, and other most noble families: inasmuch as they, in default of public merit in their house, betake themselves to the region of uncertainty, claiming demigods and divinities, merely mythical personages, as the glory of their ancestors, whose most vaunted details are incredible, and those which we can believe are an infamy.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But since our subject is a man who has maintained that each man’s nobility is to be judged of according to his own worth, and that, as forms and colours, and likewise our most celebrated and most infamous horses, are tested by their own properties, so we too ought not to be depicted in borrowed plumes; after mentioning one or two traits, which, though inherited from his ancestors, he made his own by his life, and which are specially likely to give pleasure to my hearers, I will then proceed to deal with the man himself.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Different families and individuals have different points of distinction and interest, great or small, which, like a patrimony of longer or shorter descent, come down to posterity: the distinction of his family on either side was piety, which I now proceed to display.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There was a persecution, the most frightful and severe of all; I mean, as you know, the persecution of Maximinus, which, following closely upon those which immediately preceded it, made them all seem gentle, by its excessive audacity, and by its eagerness to win the crown of violence in impiety.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It was overcome by many of our champions, who wrestled with it to the death, or well-nigh to the death, with only life enough left in them to survive their victory, and not pass away in the midst of the struggle; remaining to be trainers in virtue, living witnesses, breathing trophies, silent exhortations, among whose numerous ranks were found Basil’s paternal ancestors, upon whom, in their practice of every form of piety, that period bestowed many a fair garland.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So prepared and determined were they to bear readily all those things on account of which Christ crowns those who have imitated His struggle on our behalf.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But since their strife must needs be lawful, and the law of martyrdom alike forbids us voluntarily to go to meet it (in consideration for the persecutors, and for the weak) or to shrink from it if it comes upon us; for the former shows foolhardiness, the latter cowardice; in this respect they paid due honour to the Lawgiver; but what was their device, or rather, to what were they led by the Providence which guided them in all things?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They betook themselves to a thicket on the mountains of Pontus, of which there are many deep ones of considerable extent, with very few comrades of their flight, or attendants upon their needs.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Let others marvel at the length of time, for their flight was exceedingly prolonged, to about seven years, or a little more, and their mode of life, delicately nurtured as they were, was straitened and unusual, as may be imagined, with the discomfort of its exposure to frost and heat and rain: and the wilderness allowed no fellowship or converse with friends: a great trial to men accustomed to the attendance and honour of a numerous retinue.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But I will proceed to speak of what is still greater and more extraordinary: nor will anyone fail to credit it, save those who, in their feeble and dangerous judgment, think little of persecutions and dangers for Christ’s sake.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: These noble men, suffering from the lapse of time, and feeling a distaste for ordinary food, felt a longing for something more appetising.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They did not indeed speak as Israel did, for they were not murmurers like them, in their afflictions in the desert, after the escape from Egypt—that Egypt would have been better for them than the wilderness, in the bountiful supply of its flesh-pots, and other dainties which they had left behind them there, for the brickmaking and the clay seemed nothing to them then in their folly—but in a more pious and faithful manner.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For why, said they, is it incredible that the God of wonders, who bountifully fed in the wilderness his homeless and fugitive people, raining bread upon them, and abounding in quails, nourishing them not only with necessaries, but even with luxuries: that He, Who divided the sea, and stayed the sun, and parted the river, with all the other things that He has done; for under such circumstances the mind is wont to recur to history, and sing the praises of God’s many wonders: that He, they went on, should feed us champions of piety with dainties to-day?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Many animals which have escaped the tables of the rich, have their lairs in these mountains, and many eatable birds fly over our longing heads, any of which can surely be caught at the mere fiat of Thy will!

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: At these words, their quarry lay before them, with food come of its own accord, a complete banquet prepared without effort, stags appearing all at once from some place in the hills.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How splendid they were! how fat! how ready for the slaughter!

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It might almost be imagined that they were annoyed at not having been summoned earlier.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Some of them made signs to draw others after them, the rest followed their lead.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who pursued and drove them?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What riders?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What kind of dogs, what barking, or cry, or young men who had occupied the exits according to the rules of the chase?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They were the prisoners of prayer and righteous petition.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who has known such a hunt among men of this, or any day?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: O what a wonder!

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They were themselves stewards of the chase; what they would, was caught by the mere will to do so; what was left, they sent away to the thickets, for another meal.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The cooks were extemporised, the dinner exquisite, the guests were grateful for this wonderful foretaste of their hopes.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And hence they grew more earnest in their struggle, in return for which they had received this blessing.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such is my history.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And do thou, my persecutor, in thy admiration for legends, tell of thy huntresses, and Orions, and Actæons, those ill-fated hunters, and the hind substituted for the maiden, if any such thing rouses thee to emulation, and if we grant that this story is no legend.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The sequel of the tale is too disgraceful.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For what is the benefit of the exchange, if a maiden is saved to be taught to murder her guests, and learn to requite humanity with inhumanity?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Let this one instance, such as it is, chosen out of many, represent the rest, as far as I am concerned.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I have not related it to contribute to his reputation: for neither does the sea stand in need of the rivers which flow into it, many and great though they be, nor does the present subject of my praises need any contributions to his fair fame.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: No! my object is to exhibit the character of his ancestors, and the example before his eyes, which he so far excelled.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For if other men find it a great additional advantage to receive somewhat of their honour from their forefathers, it is a greater thing for him to have made such an addition to the original stock that the stream seems to have run uphill.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The union of his parents, cemented as it was by a community of virtue, no less than by cohabitation, was notable for many reasons, especially for generosity to the poor, for hospitality, for purity of soul as the result of self-discipline, for the dedication to God of a portion of their property, a matter not as yet so much cared for by most men, as it now has grown to be, in consequence of such previous examples, as have given distinction to it, and for all those other points, which have been published throughout Pontus and Cappadocia, to the satisfaction of many: in my opinion, however, their greatest claim to distinction is the excellence of their children.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Legend indeed has its instances of men whose children were many and beautiful, but it is practical experience which has presented to us these parents, whose own character, apart from that of their children, was sufficient for their fair fame, while the character of their children would have made them, even without their own eminence in virtue, to surpass all men by the excellence of their children.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For the attainment of distinction by one or two of their offspring might be ascribed to their nature; but when all are eminent, the honour is clearly due to those who brought them up.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This is proved by the blessed roll of priests and virgins, and of those who, when married, have allowed nothing in their union to hinder them from attaining an equal repute, and so have made the distinction between them to consist in the condition, rather than in the mode of their life.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who has not known Basil, our archbishop’s father, a great name to everyone, who attained a father’s prayer, if anyone, I will not say as no one, ever did?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he surpassed all in virtue, and was only prevented by his son from gaining the first prize.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who has not known Emmelia, whose name was a forecast of what she became, or else whose life was an exemplification of her name?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For she had a right to the name which implies gracefulness, and occupied, to speak concisely, the same place among women, as her husband among men.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So that, when it was decided that he, in whose honour we are met, should be given to men to submit to the bondage of nature, as anyone of old has been given by God for the common advantage, it was neither fitting that he should be born of other parents, nor that they should possess another son: and so the two things suitably concurred.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I have now, in obedience to the Divine law which bids us to pay all honour to parents, bestowed the firstfruits of my praises upon those whom I have commemorated, and proceed to treat of Basil himself, premising this, which I think will seem true to all who knew him, that we only need his own voice to pronounce his eulogium.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he is at once a brilliant subject for praise, and the only one whose powers of speech make him worthy of treating it.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Beauty indeed and strength and size, in which I see that most men rejoice, I concede to anyone who will—not that even in these points he was inferior to any of those men of small minds who busy themselves about the body, while he was still young, and had not yet reduced the flesh by austerity—but that I may avoid the fate of unskilful athletes, who waste their strength in vain efforts after minor objects, and so are worsted in the crucial struggle, whose results are victory and the distinction of the crown.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The praise, then, which I shall claim for him is based upon grounds which no one, I think, will consider superfluous, or beyond the scope of my oration.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I take it as admitted by men of sense, that the first of our advantages is education; and not only this our more noble form of it, which disregards rhetorical ornaments and glory, and holds to salvation, and beauty in the objects of our contemplation: but even that external culture which many Christians ill-judgingly abhor, as treacherous and dangerous, and keeping us afar from God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For as we ought not to neglect the heavens, and earth, and air, and all such things, because some have wrongly seized upon them, and honour God’s works instead of God: but to reap what advantage we can from them for our life and enjoyment, while we avoid their dangers; not raising creation, as foolish men do, in revolt against the Creator, but from the works of nature apprehending the Worker, and, as the divine apostle says, bringing into captivity every thought to Christ: and again, as we know that neither fire, nor food, nor iron, nor any other of the elements, is of itself most useful, or most harmful, except according to the will of those who use it; and as we have compounded healthful drugs from certain of the reptiles; so from secular literature we have received principles of enquiry and speculation, while we have rejected their idolatry, terror, and pit of destruction.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Nay, even these have aided us in our religion, by our perception of the contrast between what is worse and what is better, and by gaining strength for our doctrine from the weakness of theirs.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We must not then dishonour education, because some men are pleased to do so, but rather suppose such men to be boorish and uneducated, desiring all men to be as they themselves are, in order to hide themselves in the general, and escape the detection of their want of culture.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But come now, and, after this sketch of our subject and these admissions, let us contemplate the life of Basil.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In his earliest years he was swathed and fashioned, in that best and purest fashioning which the Divine David speaks of as proceeding day by day, in contrast with that of the night, under his great father, acknowledged in those days by Pontus, as its common teacher of virtue.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Under him then, as life and reason grew and rose together, our illustrious friend was educated: not boasting of a Thessalian mountain cave, as the workshop of his virtue, nor of some braggart Centaur, the tutor of the heroes of his day: nor was he taught under such tuition to shoot hares, and run down fawns, or hunt stags, or excel in war, or in breaking colts, using the same person as teacher and horse at once; nor nourished on the fabulous marrows of stags and lions, but he was trained in general education, and practised in the worship of God, and, to speak concisely, led on by elementary instructions to his future perfection.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For those who are successful in life or in letters only, while deficient in the other, seem to me to differ in nothing from one-eyed men, whose loss is great, but their deformity greater, both in their own eyes, and in those of others.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: While those who attain eminence in both alike, and are ambidextrous, both possess perfection, and pass their life with the blessedness of heaven.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This is what befell him, who had at home a model of virtue in well-doing, the very sight of which made him excellent from the first.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: As we see foals and calves skipping beside their mothers from their birth, so he too, running close beside his father in foal-like wantonness, without being left far behind in his lofty impulses toward virtue, or, if you will, sketching out and showing traces of the future beauty of his virtue, and drawing the outlines of perfection before the time of perfection arrived.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: When sufficiently trained at home, as he ought to fall short in no form of excellence, and not be surpassed by the busy bee, which gathers what is most useful from every flower, he set out for the city of Cæsarea, to take his place in the schools there, I mean this illustrious city of ours, for it was the guide and mistress of my studies, the metropolis of letters, no less than of the cities which she excels and reigns over: and if any one were to deprive her of her literary power, he would rob her of her fairest and special distinction.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Other cities take pride in other ornaments, of ancient or of recent date, that they may have something to be described or to be seen.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Letters form our distinction here, and are our badge, as if upon the field of arms or on the stage.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: His subsequent life let those detail who trained him, or enjoyed his training, as to what he was to his masters, what he was to his classmates, equalling the former, surpassing the latter in every form of culture, what renown he won in a short time from all, both of the common people, and of the leaders of the state; by showing both a culture beyond his years, and a steadfastness of character beyond his culture.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: An orator among orators, even before the chair of the rhetoricians, a philosopher among philosophers, even before the doctrines of philosophers: highest of all a priest among Christians even before the priesthood.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So much deference was paid to him in every respect by all.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Eloquence was his by-work, from which he culled enough to make it an assistance to him in Christian philosophy, since power of this kind is needed to set forth the objects of our contemplation.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For a mind which cannot express itself is like the motion of a man in a lethargy.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: His pursuit was philosophy, and breaking from the world, and fellowship with God, by concerning himself, amid things below, with things above, and winning, where all is unstable and fluctuating, the things which are stable and remain.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Thence to Byzantium, the imperial city of the East, for it was distinguished by the eminence of its rhetorical and philosophic teachers, whose most valuable lessons he soon assimilated by the quickness and force of his powers: thence he was sent by God, and by his generous craving for culture, to Athens the home of letters.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Athens, which has been to me, if to any one, a city truly of gold, and the patroness of all that is good.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For it brought me to know Basil more perfectly, though he had not been unknown to me before; and in my pursuit of letters, I attained to happiness; and in another fashion had the same experience as Saul, who, seeking his father’s asses, found a kingdom, and gained incidentally what was of more importance than the object which he had in view.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Hitherto my course has been clear, leading me in my encomiums along a level and easy, in fact, a king’s highway: henceforth I know not how to speak or whither to turn: for my task is becoming arduous.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For here I am anxious, and seize this opportunity to add from my own experience somewhat to my speech, and to dwell a little upon the recital of the causes and circumstances which originated our friendship, or to speak more strictly, our unity of life and nature.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For as our eyes are not ready to turn from attractive objects, and, if we violently tear them away, are wont to return to them again; so do we linger in our description of what is most sweet to us.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I am afraid of the difficulty of the undertaking.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I will try, however, to use all possible moderation.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if I am at all overpowered by my regret, pardon this most righteous of all feelings, the absence of which would be a great loss, in the eyes of men of feeling.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We were contained by Athens, like two branches of some river-stream, for after leaving the common fountain of our fatherland, we had been separated in our varying pursuit of culture, and were now again united by the impulsion of God no less than by our own agreement.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I preceded him by a little, but he soon followed me, to be welcomed with great and brilliant hope.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he was versed in many languages, before his arrival, and it was a great thing for either of us to outstrip the other in the attainment of some object of our study.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And I may well add, as a seasoning to any speech, a short narrative, which will be a reminder to those who know it, a source of information to those who do not.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Most of the young men at Athens in their folly are mad after rhetorical skill—not only those who are ignobly born and unknown, but even the noble and illustrious, in the general mass of young men difficult to keep under control.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They are just like men devoted to horses and exhibitions, as we see, at the horse-races; they leap, they shout, raise clouds of dust, they drive in their seats, they beat the air, (instead of the horses) with their fingers as whips, they yoke and unyoke the horses, though they are none of theirs: they readily exchange with one another drivers, horses, positions, leaders: and who are they who do this?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Often poor and needy fellows, without the means of support for a single day.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This is just how the students feel in regard to their own tutors, and their rivals, in their eagerness to increase their own numbers and thereby enrich them.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The matter is absolutely absurd and silly.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Cities, roads, harbours, mountain tops, coastlines, are seized upon—in short, every part of Attica, or of the rest of Greece, with most of the inhabitants; for even these they have divided between the rival parties.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Whenever any newcomer arrives, and falls into the hands of those who seize upon him, either by force or willingly, they observe this Attic law, of combined jest and earnest.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He is first conducted to the house of one of those who were the first to receive him, or of his friends, or kinsmen, or countrymen, or of those who are eminent in debating power, and purveyors of arguments, and therefore especially honoured among them; and their reward consists in the gain of adherents.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He is next subjected to the raillery of any one who will, with the intention I suppose, of checking the conceit of the newcomers, and reducing them to subjection at once.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The raillery is of a more insolent or argumentative kind, according to the boorishness or refinement of the railer: and the performance, which seems very fearful and brutal to those who do not know it, is to those who have experienced it very pleasant and humane: for its threats are feigned rather than real.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Next, he is conducted in procession through the market place to the bath.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The procession is formed by those who are charged with it in the young man’s honour, who arrange themselves in two ranks separated by an interval, and precede him to the bath.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But when they have approached it, they shout and leap wildly, as if possessed, shouting that they must not advance, but stay, since the bath will not admit them; and at the same time frighten the youth by furiously knocking at the doors: then allowing him to enter, they now present him with his freedom, and receive him after the bath as an equal, and one of themselves.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This they consider the most pleasant part of the ceremony, as being a speedy exchange and relief from annoyances.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: On this occasion I not only refused to put to shame my friend the great Basil, out of respect for the gravity of his character, and the ripeness of his reasoning powers, but also persuaded all the rest of the students to treat him likewise, who happened not to know him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he was from the first respected by most of them, his reputation having preceded him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The result was that he was the only one to escape the general rule, and be accorded a greater honour than belongs to a freshman’s position.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This was the prelude of our friendship.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This was the kindling spark of our union: thus we felt the wound of mutual love.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Then something of this kind happened, for I think it right not to omit even this.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I find the Armenians to be not a simple race, but very crafty and cunning.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: At this time some of his special comrades and friends, who had been intimate with him even in the early days of his father’s instruction, for they were members of his school, came up to him under the guise of friendship, but with envious, and not kindly intent, and put to him questions of a disputations rather than rational kind, trying to overwhelm him at the first onset, having known his original natural endowments, and unable to brook the honour he had then received.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For they thought it a strange thing that they who had put on their gowns, and been exercised in shouting, should not get the better of one who was a stranger and a novice.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I also, in my vain love for Athens, and trusting to their professions without perceiving their envy, when they were giving way, and turning their backs, since I was indignant that in their persons the reputation of Athens should be destroyed, and so speedily put to shame, supported the young men, and restored the argument; and by the aid of my additional weight, for in such cases a small addition makes all the difference, and, as the poet says, “made equal their heads in the fray.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But, when I perceived the secret motive of the dispute, which could no longer be kept under, and was at last clearly exposed, I at once drew back, and retired from their ranks, to range myself on his side, and made the victory decisive.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was at once delighted at what had happened, for his sagacity was remarkable, and being filled with zeal, to describe him fully in Homer’s language, he pursued in confusion with argument those valiant youths, and, smiting them with syllogisms, only ceased when they were utterly routed, and he had distinctly won the honours due to his power.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Thus was kindled again, no longer a spark, but a manifest and conspicuous blaze of friendship.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Their efforts having thus proved fruitless, while they severely blamed their own rashness, they cherished such annoyance against me that it broke out into open hostility, and a charge of treachery, not only to them, but to Athens herself: inasmuch as they had been confuted and put to shame at the first onset, by a single student, who had not even had time to gain confidence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He moreover, according to that human feeling, which makes us, when we have all at once attained to the high hopes which we have cherished, look upon their results as inferior to our expectation, he, I say, was displeased and annoyed, and could take no delight in his arrival.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was seeking for what he had expected, and called Athens an empty happiness.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I however tried to remove his annoyance, both by argumentative encounter, and by the enchantments of reasoning; alleging, as is true, that the disposition of a man cannot at once be detected, without a long time and more constant association, and that culture likewise is not made known to those who make trial of her, after a few efforts and in a short time.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In this way I restored his cheerfulness, and by this mutual experience, he was the more closely united to me.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And when, as time went on, we acknowledged our mutual affection, and that philosophy was our aim, we were all in all to one another, housemates, messmates, intimates, with one object in life, or an affection for each other ever growing warmer and stronger.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Love for bodily attractions, since its objects are fleeting, is as fleeting as the flowers of spring.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For the flame cannot survive, when the fuel is exhausted, and departs along with that which kindles it, nor does desire abide, when its incentive wastes away.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But love which is godly and under restraint, since its object is stable, not only is more lasting, but, the fuller its vision of beauty grows, the more closely does it bind to itself and to one another the hearts of those whose love has one and the same object.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This is the law of our superhuman love.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I feel that I am being unduly borne away, and I know not how to enter upon this point, yet I cannot restrain myself from describing it.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For if I have omitted anything, it seems, immediately afterwards, of pressing importance, and of more consequence than what I had preferred to mention.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if any one would carry me tyrannically forward, I become like the polyps, which when they are being dragged from their holes, cling with their suckers to the rocks, and cannot be detached, until the last of these has had exerted upon it its necessary share of force.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If then you give me leave, I have my request, if not I must take it from myself.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such were our feelings for each other, when we had thus supported, as Pindar has it, our “well-built chamber with pillars of gold,” as we advanced under the united influences of God’s grace and our own affection.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Oh! how can I mention these things without tears.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We were impelled by equal hopes, in a pursuit especially obnoxious to envy, that of letters.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Yet envy we knew not, and emulation was of service to us.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We struggled, not each to gain the first place for himself, but to yield it to the other; for we made each other’s reputation to be our own.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We seemed to have one soul, inhabiting two bodies.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if we must not believe those whose doctrine is “All things are in all;” yet in our case it was worthy of belief, so did we live in and with each other.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The sole business of both of us was virtue, and living for the hopes to come, having retired from this world, before our actual departure hence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: With a view to this, were directed all our life and actions, under the guidance of the commandment, as we sharpened upon each other our weapons of virtue; and if this is not a great thing for me to say, being a rule and standard to each other, for the distinction between what was right and what was not.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Our associates were not the most dissolute, but the most sober of our comrades; not the most pugnacious, but the most peaceable, whose intimacy was most profitable: knowing that it is more easy to be tainted with vice, than to impart virtue; just as we can more readily be infected with a disease, than bestow health.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Our most cherished studies were not the most pleasant, but the most excellent; this being one means of forming young minds in a virtuous or vicious mould.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Two ways were known to us, the first of greater value, the second of smaller consequence: the one leading to our sacred buildings and the teachers there, the other to secular instructors.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: All others we left to those who would pursue them—to feasts, theatres, meetings, banquets.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For nothing is in my opinion of value, save that which leads to virtue and to the improvement of its devotees.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Different men have different names, derived from their fathers, their families, their pursuits, their exploits: we had but one great business and name—to be and to be called Christians of which we thought more than Gyges of the turning of his ring, if this is not a legend, on which depended his Lydian sovereignty: or than Midas did of the gold through which he perished, in answer to his prayer that all he had might turn to gold—another Phrygian legend.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For why should I speak of the arrow of the Hyperborean Abaris, or of the Argive Pegasus, to whom flight through the air was not of such consequence as was to us our rising to God, through the help of, and with each other?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Hurtful as Athens was to others in spiritual things, and this is of no slight consequence to the pious, for the city is richer in those evil riches—idols—than the rest of Greece, and it is hard to avoid being carried along with their devotees and adherents, yet we, our minds being closed up and fortified against this, suffered no injury.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: On the contrary, strange as it may seem, we were thus the more confirmed in the faith, from our perception of their trickery and unreality, which led us to despise these divinities in the very home of their worship.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if there is, or is believed to be, a river flowing with fresh water through the sea, or an animal which can dance in fire, the consumer of all things, such were we among all our comrades.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And, best of all, we were surrounded by a far from ignoble band, under his instruction and guidance, and delighting in the same objects, as we ran on foot beside that Lydian car, his own course and disposition: and so we became famous, not only among our own teachers and comrades, but even throughout Greece, and especially in the eyes of its most distinguished men.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We even passed beyond its boundaries, as was made clear by the evidence of many.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For our instructors were known to all who knew Athens, and all who knew them, knew us, as the subject of conversation, being actually looked upon, or heard of by report, as an illustrious pair.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Orestes and Pylades were in their eyes nothing to us, or the sons of Molione, the wonders of the Homeric scroll, celebrated for their union in misfortune, and their splendid driving, as they shared in reins and whip alike.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But I have been unawares betrayed into praising myself, in a manner I would not have allowed in another.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And it is no wonder that I gained here in some advantage from his friendship, and that, as in life he aided me in virtue, so since his departure he has contributed to my renown.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But I must return to my proper course.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who possessed such a degree of the prudence of old age, even before his hair was gray?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Since it is by this that Solomon defines old age.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who was so respectful to both old and young, not only of our contemporaries, but even of those who long preceded him?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who, owing to his character, was less in need of education?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Yet who, even with his character, was so imbued with learning?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What branch of learning did he not traverse; and that with unexampled success, passing through all, as no one else passed through any one of them: and attaining such eminence in each, as if it had been his sole study?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The two great sources of power in the arts and sciences, ability and application, were in him equally combined.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For, because of the pains he took, he had but little need of natural quickness, and his natural quickness made it unnecessary for him to take pains; and such was the cooperation and unity of both, that it was hard to see for which of the two he was more remarkable.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who had such power in Rhetoric, which breathes with the might of fire, different as his disposition was from that of rhetoricians?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who in Grammar, which perfects our tongues in Greek and compiles history, and presides over metres and legislates for poems?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who in Philosophy, that really lofty and high reaching science, whether practical and speculative, or in that part of it whose oppositions and struggles are concerned with logical demonstrations; which is called Dialectic, and in which it was more difficult to elude his verbal toils, if need required, than to escape from the Labyrinths?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Of Astronomy, Geometry, and numerical proportion he had such a grasp, that he could not be baffled by those who are clever in such sciences: excessive application to them he despised, as useless to those whose desire is godliness: so that it is possible to admire what he chose more than what he neglected, or what he neglected more than what he chose.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Medicine, the result of philosophy and laboriousness, was rendered necessary for him by his physical delicacy, and his care of the sick.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: From these beginnings he attained to a mastery of the art, not only in its empirical and practical branches, but also in its theory and principles.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But what are these, illustrious though they be, compared with the moral discipline of the man?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: To those who have had experience of him, Minos and Rhadamanthus were mere trifles, whom the Greeks thought worthy of the meadows of Asphodel and the Elysian plains, which are their representations of our Paradise, derived from those books of Moses which are also ours, for though their terms are different, this is what they refer to under other names.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such was the case, and his galleon was laden with all the learning attainable by the nature of man; for beyond Cadiz there is no passage.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There was left no other need but that of rising to a more perfect life, and grasping those hopes upon which we were agreed.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The day of our departure was at hand, with its attendant speeches of farewell, and of escort, its invitations to return, its lamentations, embraces and tears.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For there is nothing so painful to any one, as is separation from Athens and one another, to those who have been comrades there.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: On that occasion was seen a piteous spectacle, worthy of record.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Around us were grouped our fellow students and classmates and some of our teachers, protesting amid entreaties, violence, and persuasion, that, whatever happened, they would not let us go; saying and doing everything that men in distress could do.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And here I will bring an accusation against myself, and also, daring though it be, against that divine and irreproachable soul.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he, by detailing the reasons of his anxiety to return home, was able to prevail over their desire to retain him, and they were compelled, though with reluctance, to agree to his departure.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But I was left behind at Athens, partly, to say the truth, because I had been prevailed on—partly because he had betrayed me, having been persuaded to forsake and hand over to his captors one who refused to forsake him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: A thing incredible, before it happened.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For it was like cutting one body into two, to the destruction of either part, or the severance of two bullocks who have shared the same manger and the same yoke, amid pitiable bellowings after one another in protest against the separation.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: However, my loss was not of long duration, for I could not long bear to be seen in piteous plight, nor to have to account to every one for our separation: so, after a brief stay at Athens, my longing desire made me, like the horse in Homer, to burst the bonds of those who restrained me, and prancing o’er the plains, rush to my mate.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Upon our return, after a slight indulgence to the world and the stage, sufficient to gratify the general desire, not from any inclination to theatrical display, we soon became independent, and, after being promoted from the rank of beardless boys to that of men, made bold advances along the road of philosophy, for though no longer together, since envy would not allow this, we were united by our eager desire.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The city of Cæsarea took possession of him, as a second founder and patron, but in course of time he was occasionally absent, as a matter of necessity due to our separation, and with a view to our determined course of philosophy.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Dutiful attendance on my aged parents, and a succession of misfortunes kept me apart from him, perhaps without right or justice, but so it was.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And to this cause I am inclined to ascribe all the inconsistency and difficulty which have befallen my life, and the hindrances in the way of philosophy, which have been unworthy of my desire and purpose.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But as for my fate, let it lead whither God pleases, only may its course be the better for his intercessions.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: As regards himself, the manifold love of God toward man, and His providential care for our race did, after shewing forth his merits under many intervening circumstances with ever greater brilliancy, set him up as a conspicuous and celebrated light for the Church, by advancing him to the holy thrones of the priesthood, to blaze forth, through the single city of Cæsarea, to the whole world.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And in what manner?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Not by precipitate advancement, nor by at once cleansing and making him wise, as is the wont of many present candidates for preferment: but bestowing upon him the honour in the due order of spiritual advancement.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For I do not praise the disorder and irregularity which sometimes exist among us, even in those who preside over the sanctuary.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I do not venture, nor is it just, to accuse them all.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I approve the nautical custom, which first gives the oar to the future steersman, and afterward leads him to the stern, and entrusts him with the command, and seats him at the helm, only after a long course of striking the sea and observing the winds.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: As is the case again in military affairs: private, captain, general.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This order is the best and most advantageous for their subordinates.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if it were so in our case, it would be of great service.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But, as it is, there is a danger of the holiest of all offices being the most ridiculous among us.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For promotion depends not upon virtue, but upon villany; and the sacred thrones fall not to the most Worthy, but to the most powerful.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Samuel, the seer into futurity, is among the prophets: but Saul, the rejected one, is also there.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Rehoboam, the son of Solomon, is among the kings, but so also is Jeroboam, the slave and apostate.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And there is not a physician, or a painter who has not first studied the nature of diseases, or mixed many colours, or practised drawing: but a prelate is easily found, without laborious training, with a reputation of recent date, being sown and springing up in a moment, as the legend of the giants goes.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We manufacture those who are holy in a day, and bid those to be wise, who have had no instruction, and have contributed nothing before to their dignity, except the will.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So one man is content with an inferior position, and abides in his low estate, who is worthy of a lofty one, and has meditated much on the inspired words, and has reduced the flesh by many laws into subjection to the spirit: while the other haughtily takes precedence, and raises his eyebrow over his betters, and does not tremble at his position, nor is he appalled at the sight, seeing the disciplined man beneath him; and wrongly supposes himself to be his superior in wisdom as well as in rank, having lost his senses under the influence of his position.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Not so our great and illustrious Basil.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In this grace, as in all others, he was a public example.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he first read to the people the sacred books, while already able to expound them, nor did he deem himself worthy of this rank in the sanctuary, and thus proceeded to praise the Lord in the seat of the Presbyters, and next in that of the Bishops, attaining the office neither by stealth nor by violence, instead of seeking for the honour, being sought for by it, and receiving it not as a human favour, but as from God and divine.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The account of his bishopric must be deferred: over his subordinate ministry let us linger a while, for indeed it had almost escaped me, in the midst of my discourse.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There arose a disagreement between him and his predecessor in the rule over this Church: its source and character it is best to pass over in silence, yet it arose.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was a man in other respects far from ignoble, and admirable for his piety, as was proved by the persecution of that time, and the opposition to him, yet his feeling against Basil was one to which men are liable.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For Momus seizes not only upon the common herd, but on the best of men, so that it belongs to God alone to be utterly uninfluenced by and proof against such feelings.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: All the more eminent and wise portion of the Church was roused against him, if those are wiser than the majority who have separated themselves from the world and consecrated their life to God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I mean the Nazarites of our day, and those who devote themselves to such pursuits.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They were annoyed that their chief should be neglected, insulted, and rejected, and they ventured upon a most dangerous proceeding.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They determined to revolt and break off from the body of the Church, which admits of no faction, severing along with themselves no small fraction of the people, both of the lower ranks, and of those of position.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This was most easy, owing to three very strong reasons.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In the first place, the man was held in repute, beyond any other, I think, of the philosophers of our time, and able, if he wished, to inspire with courage the conspirators.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Next, his opponent was suspected by the city, in consequence of the tumult which accompanied his institution, of having obtained his preferment in an arbitrary manner, not according to the laws and canons.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Also there were present some of the bishops of the West, drawing to themselves all the orthodox members of the Church.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What then did our noble friend, the disciple of the Peaceable One?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It was not his habit to resist his traducers or partisans, nor was it his part to fight, or rend the body of the Church, which was from other reasons the subject of attack, and hardly bestead, from the great power of the heretics.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: With my advice and earnest encouragement on the point, he set out from the place with me into Pontus, and presided over the abodes of contemplation there.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He himself too founded one worthy of mention, as he welcomed the desert together with Elijah and John, those professors of austerity; thinking this to be more profitable for him than to form any design in reference to the present juncture unworthy of his philosophy, and to ruin in a time of storm the straight course which he was making, where the surges of disputation were lulled to a calm.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Yet wonderfully philosophic though his retirement was, we shall find his return still more wonderful.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For thus it was.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: While we were thus engaged, there suddenly arose a cloud full of hail, with destructive roar, overwhelming every Church upon which it burst and seized: an Emperor, most fond of gold and most hostile to Christ, infected with these two most serious diseases, insatiate avarice and blasphemy; a persecutor in succession to the persecutor, and, in succession to the apostate, not indeed an apostate, though no better to Christians, or rather, to the more devout and pure party of Christians, who worship the Trinity, which I call the only true devotion and saving doctrine.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For we do not measure out the Godhead into portions, nor banish from Itself by unnatural estrangements the one and unapproachable Nature; nor cure one evil by another, destroying the godless confusion of Sabellius by a more impious severance and division; which was the error of Arius, whose name declares his madness, the disturber and destroyer of a great part of the Church.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he did not honour the Father, by dishonouring His offspring with his unequal degrees of Godhead.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But we recognize one glory of the Father, the equality of the Only-begotten; and one glory of the Son, that of the Spirit.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And we hold that, to subordinate any of the Three, is to destroy the whole.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For we worship and acknowledge Them as Three in their properties, but One in their Godhead.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He however had no such idea, being unable to look up, but being debased by those who led him, he dared to debase along with himself even the Nature of the Godhead, and became a wicked creature reducing Majesty to bondage, and aligning with creation the uncreated and timeless Nature.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such was his mind, and with such impiety he took the field against us.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For we must consider it to be nothing else than a barbaric inroad which, instead of destroying walls, cities and houses, and other things of little worth, made with hands and capable of restoration, spent its ravages upon men’s souls.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: A worthy army joined in his assault, the evil rulers of the Churches, the bitter governors of his world-wide Empire.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Some of the Churches they now held, some they were assaulting, others they hoped to gain by the already exercised influence of the Emperor, and the violence which he threatened.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But in their purpose of perverting our own, their confidence was specially based on the smallness of mind of those whom I have mentioned, the inexperience of our prelate, and the infirmities which prevailed among us.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The struggle would be fierce: the zeal of numerous troops was far from ignoble, but their array was weak, from the want of a leader and strategist to contend for them with the might of the Word and of the Spirit.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What then did this noble and magnanimous and truly Christ-loving soul?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: No need of many words to urge his presence and aid.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: At once when he saw me on my mission, for the struggle on behalf of the faith was common to us both, he yielded to my entreaty; and decided by a most excellent distinction, based on spiritual reasons, that the time for punctiliousness (if indeed we may give way to such feelings at all) is a time of security, but that forbearance is required in the hour of necessity.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He immediately returned with me from Pontus, and as a zealous volunteer took his place in the fight for the endangered truth, and devoted himself to the service of his mother, the Church.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Did then his actual efforts fall short of his preliminary zeal?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Were they directed by courage, but not by prudence, or by skill, while he shrank from danger?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Or, in spite of their unexampled perfection on all these points, was there left in him some trace of irritation?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Far from it.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was at once completely reconciled, and took part in every plan and effort.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He removed all the thorns and stumbling blocks which were in our way, upon which the enemy relied in their attack upon us.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He took hold of one, grasped another, thrust away a third.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He became to some a stout wall and rampart, to others an axe breaking the rock in pieces, or a fire among the thorns, as the divine Scripture says, easily destroying those fagots who were insulting the Godhead.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if his Barnabas, who speaks and records these things, was of service to Paul in the struggle, it is to Paul that thanks are due, for choosing and making him his comrade in the strife.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Thus the enemy failed, and, base men as they were, for the first time were then basely put to shame and worsted, learning not to be ready to despise the Cappadocians, of all men in the world, whose special qualities are firmness in the faith, and loyal devotion to the Trinity; to Whom is due their unity and strength, and from Whom they receive an even greater and stronger assistance than they are able to give.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Basil’s next business and purpose was to conciliate the prelate, to allay suspicion, to persuade all men that the irritation which had been felt was due to the temptation and effort of the Evil one, in his envy of virtuous concord: carefully complying with the laws of obedience and spiritual order.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Accordingly he visited him, with instruction and advice.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: While obedient to his wishes, he was everything to him, a good counsellor, a skilful assistant, an expounder of the Divine Will, a guide of conduct, a staff for his old age, a support of the faith, most trusty of those within, most practical of those without, in a word, as much inclined to goodwill, as he had been thought to hostility.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And so the power of the Church came into his hands almost, if not quite, to an equal degree with the occupant of the see.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For in return for his good-will, he was requited with authority.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And their harmony and combination of power was wonderful.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The one was the leader of the people, the other of their leader, like a lion-keeper, skilfully soothing the possessor of power.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For, having been recently installed in the see, and still somewhat under the influence of the world, and not yet furnished with the things of the Spirit, in the midst of the eddying tide of enemies assaulting the Church, he was in need of some one to take him by the hand and support him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Accordingly he accepted the alliance, and imagined himself the conqueror of one who had conquered him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Of his care for and protection of the Church, there are many other tokens; his boldness towards the governors and other most powerful men in the city: the decisions of disputes, accepted without hesitation, and made effective by his simple word, his inclination being held to be decisive: his support of the needy, most of them in spiritual, not a few also in physical distress: for this also often influences the soul and reduces it to subjection by its kindness; the support of the poor, the entertainment of strangers, the care of maidens; legislation written and unwritten for the monastic life: arrangements of prayers, adornments of the sanctuary, and other ways in which the true man of God, working for God, would benefit the people: one being especially important and noteworthy.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There was a famine, the most severe one ever recorded.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The city was in distress, and there was no source of assistance, or relief for the calamity.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For maritime cities are able to bear such times of need without difficulty, by an exchange of their own products for what is imported: but an inland city like ours can neither turn its superfluity to profit, nor supply its need, by either disposing of what we have, or importing what we have not: but the hardest part of all such distress is, the insensibility and insatiability of those who possess supplies.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For they watch their opportunities, and turn the distress to profit, and thrive upon misfortune: heeding not that he who shows mercy to the poor, lendeth to the Lord, nor that he that withholdeth corn, the people shall curse him: nor any other of the promises to the philanthropic, and threats against the inhuman.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But they are too insatiate, in their ill-judged policy; for while they shut up their bowels against their fellows, they shut up those of God against themselves, forgetting that their need of Him is greater than others’ need of them.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such are the buyers and sellers of corn, who neither respect their fellows, nor are thankful to God, from Whom comes what they have, while others are straitened.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He indeed could neither rain bread from heaven by prayer, to nourish an escaped people in the wilderness, nor supply fountains of food without cost from the depth of vessels which are filled by being emptied, and so, by an amazing return for her hospitality, support one who supported him; nor feed thousands of men with five loaves whose very fragments were a further supply for many tables.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: These were the works of Moses and Elijah, and my God, from Whom they too derived their power.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Perhaps also they were characteristic of their time and its circumstances: since signs are for unbelievers not for those who believe.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But he did devise and execute with the same faith things which correspond to them, and tend in the same direction.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For by his word and advice he opened the stores of those who possessed them, and so, according to the Scripture dealt food to the hungry, and satisfied the poor with bread, and fed them in the time of dearth, and filled the hungry souls with good things.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And in what way? for this is no slight addition to his praise.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He gathered together the victims of the famine with some who were but slightly recovering from it, men and women, infants, old men, every age which was in distress, and obtaining contributions of all sorts of food which can relieve famine, set before them basins of soup and such meat as was found preserved among us, on which the poor live.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
