verses.text: Then, imitating the ministry of Christ, Who, girded with a towel, did not disdain to wash the disciples’ feet, using for this purpose the aid of his own servants, and also of his fellow servants, he attended to the bodies and souls of those who needed it, combining personal respect with the supply of their necessity, and so giving them a double relief.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such was our young furnisher of corn, and second Joseph: though of him we can say somewhat more.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For the one made a gain from the famine, and bought up Egypt in his philanthropy, by managing the time of plenty with a view to the time of famine, turning to account the dreams of others for that purpose.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But the other’s services were gratuitous, and his succour of the famine gained no profit, having only one object, to win kindly feelings by kindly treatment, and to gain by his rations of corn the heavenly blessings.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Further he provided the nourishment of the Word, and that more perfect bounty and distribution, which is really heavenly and from on high—if the word be that bread of angels, wherewith souls are fed and given to drink, who are a hungered for God, and seek for a food which does not pass away or fail, but abides forever.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This food he, who was the poorest and most needy man whom I have known, supplied in rich abundance to the relief not of a famine of bread, nor of a thirst for water, but a longing for that Word which is really lifegiving and nourishing, and causes to grow to spiritual manhood him who is duly fed thereon.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: After these and similar actions—why need I stay to mention them all?—when the prelate whose name betokened his godliness had passed away, having sweetly breathed his last in Basil’s arms, he was raised to the lofty throne of a Bishop, not without difficulty or without the envious struggles of the prelates of his native land, on whose side were found the greatest scoundrels of the city.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But the Holy Spirit must needs win the day—and indeed the victory was decisive.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For He brought from a distance, to anoint him, men illustrious and zealous for godliness, and with them the new Abraham, our Patriarch, I mean my father, in regard to whom an extraordinary thing happened.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For, failing as he was from the number of his years, and worn away almost to his last breath by disease, he ventured on the journey to give assistance by his vote, relying on the aid of the Spirit.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In brief, he was placed in his litter, as a corpse is laid in its tomb, to return in the freshness and strength of youth, with head erect, having been strengthened by the imposition of hands and unction, and, it is not too much to say by the head of him who was anointed.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This must be added to the instances of old time, which prove that labour bestows health, zealous purpose raises the dead, and old age leaps up when anointed by the Spirit.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Having thus been deemed worthy of the office of prelate, as it is seemly that men should who have lived such a life, and won such favour and consideration, he did not disgrace, by his subsequent conduct, either his own philosophy, or the hopes of those who had trusted him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But he ever so far surpassed himself as he has been shown hitherto to have surpassed others, his ideas on this point being most excellent and philosophic.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he held that, while it is virtuous in a private individual to avoid vice, and be to some extent good, it is a vice in a chief and ruler, especially in such an office, to fail to surpass by far the majority of men, and by constant progress to make his virtue correspond to his dignity and throne: for it is difficult for one in high position to attain the mean, and by his eminence in virtue raise up his people to the golden mean.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Or rather to treat this question more satisfactorily, I think that the result is the same as I see in the case of our Saviour, and of every specially wise man, I fancy, when He was with us in that form which surpassed us and yet is ours.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For He also, the gospel says, increased in wisdom and favour, as well as in stature, not that these qualities in Him were capable of growth: for how could that which was perfect from the first become more perfect, but that they were gradually disclosed and displayed?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So I think that the virtue of Basil, without being itself increased, obtained at this time a wider exercise, since his power provided him with more abundant material.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He first of all made it plain that his office had been bestowed upon him, not by human favour, but by the gift of God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This will also be shown by my conduct.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For in what philosophic research did he not, about that time, join with me?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So every one thought that I should run to meet him after what had happened, and show my delight at it (as would, perhaps, have been the case with any one else) and claim a share in his authority, rather than rule beside him, according to the inferences they drew from our friendship.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But, in my exceeding anxiety to avoid the annoyance and jealousy of the time, and specially since his position was still a painful and troubled one, I remained at home, and forcibly restrained my eager desire, while, though he blamed me, Basil accepted my excuse.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And when, on my subsequent arrival, I refused, for the same reason the honour of this chair, and a dignified position among the Presbyters, he kindly refrained from blaming, nay he praised me, preferring to be charged with pride by a small clique, in their ignorance of our policy, rather than do anything contrary to reason and his own resolutions.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And indeed, how could a man have better shown his soul to be superior to all fawning and flattery, and his single object to be the law of right, than by thus treating me, whom he acknowledged as among the first of his friends and associates?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: His next task was to appease, and allay by magnanimous treatment, the opposition to himself: and that without any trace of flattery or servility, but in a most chivalrous and magnanimous way; with a view, not merely to present exigencies, but also to the fostering of future obedience.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For, seeing that, while tenderness leads to laxity and slackness, severity gives rise to stubbornness and self-will, he was able to avoid the dangers of each course by a combination of both, blending his correction with consideration, and gentleness with firmness, influencing men in most cases principally by his conduct rather than by argument: not enslaving them by art, but winning them by good nature, and attracting them by the sparing use, rather than by the constant exercise, of his power.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And, most important of all, they were brought to recognize the superiority of his intellect and the inaccessibility of his virtue, to consider their only safety to consist in being on his side and under his command, their sole danger to be in opposition to him, and to think that to differ from him involved estrangement from God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Thus they willingly yielded and surrendered, submitting themselves, as if in a thunder-clap, and hastening to anticipate each other with their excuses, and exchange the intensity of their hostility for an equal intensity of goodwill, and advance in virtue, which they found to be the one really effective defence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The few exceptions to this conduct were passed by and neglected, because their ill-nature was incurable, and they expended their powers in wearing out themselves, as rust consumes itself together with the iron on which it feeds.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Affairs at home being now settled to his mind, in a way that faithless men who did not know him would have thought impossible, his designs became greater and took a loftier range.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For, while all others had their eyes on the ground before them, and directed attention to their own immediate concerns, and, if these were safe, troubled themselves no further, being incapable of any great and chivalrous design or undertaking; he, moderate as he was in all other respects, could not be moderate in this, but with head erect, casting his mental eye about him, took in the whole world over which the word of salvation has made its way.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And when he saw the great heritage of God, purchased by His own words and laws and sufferings, the holy nation, the royal priesthood, in such evil plight that it was torn asunder into ten thousand opinions and errors: and the vine brought out of Egypt and transplanted, the Egypt of impious and dark ignorance, which had grown to such beauty and boundless size that the whole earth was covered with the shadow of it, while it overtopped mountains and cedars, now being ravaged by that wicked wild boar, the devil, he could not content himself with quietly lamenting the misfortune, and merely lifting up his hands to God, and seeking from Him the dispersion of the pressing misfortunes, while he himself was asleep, but felt bound to come to her aid at some expense to himself.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For what could be more distressing than this calamity, or call more loudly on one whose eyes were raised aloft for exertions on behalf of the common weal?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The good or ill success of an individual is of no consequence to the community, but that of the community involves of necessity the like condition of the individual.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: With this idea and purpose, he who was the guardian and patron of the community (and, as Solomon says with truth, a perceptive heart is a moth to the bones, unsensitiveness is cheerily confident, while a sympathetic disposition is a source of pain, and constant consideration wastes away the heart), he, I say, was consequently in agony and distress from many wounds; like Jonah and David, he wished in himself to die and gave not sleep to his eyes, nor slumber to his eyelids, he expended what was left of his flesh upon his reflections, until he discovered a remedy for the evil: and sought for aid from God and man, to stay the general conflagration, and dissipate the gloom which was lowering over us.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: One of his devices was of the greatest service.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: After a period of such recollection as was possible, and private spiritual conference, in which, after considering all human arguments, and penetrating into all the deep things of the Scriptures, he drew up a sketch of pious doctrine, and by wrestling with and attacking their opposition he beat off the daring assaults of the heretics: overthrowing in hand to hand struggles by word of mouth those who came to close quarters, and striking those at a distance by arrows winged with ink, which is in no wise inferior to inscriptions on tablets; not giving directions for one small nation only like that of the Jews, concerning meats and drinks, temporary sacrifices, and purifications of the flesh; but for every nation and part of the world, concerning the Word of truth, the source of our salvation.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Again, since unreasoning action and unpractical reasoning are alike ineffectual, he added to his reasoning the succour which comes from action; he paid visits, sent messages, gave interviews, instructed, reproved, rebuked, threatened, reproached, undertook the defence of nations, cities and individuals, devising every kind of succour, and procuring from every source specifics for disease: a second Bezaleel, an architect of the Divine tabernacle, applying every material and art to the work, and combining all in a harmonious and surpassing beauty.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Why need I enter into further detail?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We were assailed again by the Anti-Christian Emperor, that tyrant of the faith, with more abundant impiety and a hotter onset, inasmuch as the dispute must be with a stronger antagonist, like that unclean and evil spirit, who when sent forth upon his wanderings from man, returns to take up his abode in him again with a greater number of spirits, as we have heard in the Gospels.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This spirit he imitated, both in renewing the contest in which he had formerly been worsted, and in adding to his original efforts.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He thought that it was a strange and insufferable thing that he, who ruled over so many nations and had won so much renown, and reduced under the power of impiety all those round about him, and overcome every adversary, should be publicly worsted by a single man, and a single city, and so incur the ridicule not only of those patrons of ungodliness by whom he was led, but also, as he supposed, of all men.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It is said that the King of Persia, on his expedition into Greece, was not only urged to immoderate threats, by elation at the numbers of every race of men which in his wrath and pride he was leading against them: but thought to terrify them the more, by making them afraid of him, in consequence of his novel treatment of the elements.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: A strange land and sea were heard of, the work of the new creator; and an army which sailed over the dry land, and marched over the ocean, while islands were carried off, and the sea was scourged, and all the other mad proceedings of that army and expedition, which, though they struck terror into the ignoble, were ridiculous in the eyes of men of brave and steadfast hearts.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There was no need of anything of this kind in the expedition against us, but what was still worse and more harmful, this was what the Emperor was reported to say and do.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He stretched forth his mouth unto heaven, speaking blasphemy against the most High, and his tongue went through the world.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Excellently did the inspired David before our days thus describe him who made heaven to stoop to earth, and reckoned with the creation that supermundane nature, which the creation cannot even contain, even though in kindness to man it did to some extent come among us, in order to draw to itself us who were lying upon the ground.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Furious indeed were his first acts of wantonness, more furious still his final efforts against us.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What shall I speak of first?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Exiles, banishments, confiscations, open and secret plots, persuasion, where time allowed, violence, where persuasion was impossible.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Those who clung to the orthodox faith, as we did, were extruded from their churches; others were intruded, who agreed with the Imperial soul-destroying doctrines, and begged for testimonials of impiety, and subscribed to statements still harder than these.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Burnings of Presbyters at sea, impious generals, not those who conquered the Persians, or subdued the Scythians, or reduced any other barbaric nation, but those who assailed churches, and danced in triumph upon altars, and defiled the unbloody sacrifices with the blood of man and victims, and offered insult to the modesty of virgins.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: With what object?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The extrusion of the Patriarch Jacob, and the intrusion in his place of Esau, who was hated, even before his birth.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This is the description of his first acts of wantonness, the mere recollection and mention of which even now, rouses the tears of most of us.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Accordingly, when, after passing through all quarters, he made his attack in order to enslave this impregnable and formidable mother of the Churches, the only still remaining unquenched spark of the truth, he discovered that he had been for the first time ill advised.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he was driven back like a missile which strikes upon some stronger body, and recoiled like a broken hawser.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such was the prelate of the Church that he met with, such was the bulwark by which his efforts were broken and dissipated.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Other particulars may be heard from those who tell and recount them, from their own experience—and none of those who recount them is destitute of this full experience.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But all must be filled with admiration who are aware of the struggles of that time, the assaults, the promises, the threats, the commissioners sent before him to try to prevail upon us, men of judicial and military rank, men from the harem, who are men among women, women among men, whose only manliness consisted in their impiety, and being incapable of natural licentiousness, commit fornication in the only way they can, with their tongues; the chief cook Nebuzaradan, who threatened us with the weapons of his art, and was despatched by his own fire.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But what especially excites my wonder, and what I could not, even if I would, pass by, I will describe as concisely as possible.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who has not heard of the prefect of those days, who, for his own part, treated us with such excessive arrogance, having himself been admitted, or perhaps committed, to baptism by the other party; and strove by exceeding the letter of his instructions, and gratifying his master in every particular, to guarantee and preserve his own possession of power.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Though he raged against the Church, and assumed a lion-like aspect, and roared like a lion till most men dared not approach him, yet our noble prelate was brought into or rather entered his court, as if bidden to a feast, instead of to a trial.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How can I fully describe, either the arrogance of the prefect or the prudence with which it was met by the Saint.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “What is the meaning, Sir Basil,” he said, addressing him by name, and not as yet deigning to term him Bishop, “of your daring, as no other dares, to resist and oppose so great a potentate?”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “In what respect?” said our noble champion, “and in what does my rashness consist?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For this I have yet to learn.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “In refusing to respect the religion of your Sovereign, when all others have yielded and submitted themselves?”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “Because,” said he, “this is not the will of my real Sovereign; nor can I, who am the creature of God, and bidden myself to be God, submit to worship any creature.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “And what do we,” said the prefect, “seem to you to be?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Are we, who give you this injunction, nothing at all?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What do you say to this?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Is it not a great thing to be ranged with us as your associates?”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “You are, I will not deny it,” said he, “a prefect, and an illustrious one, yet not of more honour than God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And to be associated with you is a great thing, certainly; for you are yourself the creature of God; but so it is to be associated with any other of my subjects.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For faith, and not personal importance, is the distinctive mark of Christianity.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Then indeed the prefect became excited, and rose from his seat, boiling with rage, and making use of harsher language.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “What?” said he, “have you no fear of my authority?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “Fear of what?” said Basil, “How could it affect me?”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Of any one of the resources of my power.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “What are these?” said Basil, “pray, inform me.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “Confiscation, banishment, torture, death.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “Have you no other threat?” said he, “for none of these can reach me.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “How indeed is that?” said the prefect.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “Because,” he replied, “a man who has nothing, is beyond the reach of confiscation; unless you demand my tattered rags, and the few books, which are my only possessions.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Banishment is impossible for me, who am confined by no limit of place, counting my own neither the land where I now dwell, nor all of that into which I may be hurled; or, rather, counting it all God’s, whose guest and dependent I am.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: As for tortures, what hold can they have upon one whose body has ceased to be?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Unless you mean the first stroke, for this alone is in your power.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Death is my benefactor, for it will send me the sooner to God, for Whom I live, and exist, and have all but died, and to Whom I have long been hastening.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Amazed at this language, the prefect said, “No one has ever yet spoken thus, and with such boldness, to Modestus.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: “Why, perhaps,” said Basil, “you have not met with a Bishop, or in his defence of such interests he would have used precisely the same language.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For we are modest in general, and submissive to every one, according to the precept of our law.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We may not treat with haughtiness even any ordinary person, to say nothing of so great a potentate.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But where the interests of God are at stake, we care for nothing else, and make these our sole object.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Fire and sword and wild beasts, and rakes which tear the flesh, we revel in, and fear them not.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: You may further insult and threaten us, and do whatever you will, to the full extent of your power.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The Emperor himself may hear this—that neither by violence nor persuasion will you bring us to make common cause with impiety, not even though your threats become still more terrible.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: At the close of this colloquy, the prefect, having been convinced by the attitude of Basil, that he was absolutely impervious to threats and influence, dismissed him from the court, his former threatening manner being replaced by somewhat of respect and deference.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He himself with all speed obtained an audience of the Emperor, and said: “We have been worsted, Sire, by the prelate of this Church.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He is superior to threats, invincible in argument, uninfluenced by persuasion.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We must make trial of some more feeble character; and in this case resort to open violence, or submit to the disregard of our threatenings.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Hereupon the Emperor, forced by the praises of Basil to condemn his own conduct (for even an enemy can admire a man’s excellence), would not allow violence to be used against him: and, like iron, which is softened by fire, yet still remains iron, though turned from threatening to admiration, would not enter into communion with him, being prevented by shame from changing his course, but sought to justify his conduct by the most plausible excuse he could, as the sequel will show.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he entered the Church attended by the whole of his train; it was the festival of the Epiphany, and the Church was crowded, and, by taking his place among the people, he made a profession of unity.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The occurrence is not to be lightly passed over.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Upon his entrance he was struck by the thundering roll of the Psalms, by the sea of heads of the congregation, and by the angelic rather than human order which pervaded the sanctuary and its precincts: while Basil presided over his people, standing erect, as the Scripture says of Samuel, with body and eyes and mind undisturbed, as if nothing new had happened, but fixed upon God and the sanctuary, as if, so to say, he had been a statue, while his ministers stood around him in fear and reverence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: At this sight, and it was indeed a sight unparalleled, overcome by human weakness, his eyes were affected with dimness and giddiness, his mind with dread.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This was as yet unnoticed by most people.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But when he had to offer the gifts at the Table of God, which he must needs do himself, since no one would, as usual, assist him, because it was uncertain whether Basil would admit him, his feelings were revealed.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he was staggering, and had not some one in the sanctuary reached out a hand to steady his tottering steps, he would have sunk to the ground in a lamentable fall.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So much for this.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: As for the wisdom of his conference with the Emperor, who, in his quasi-communion with us entered within the veil to see and speak to him, as he had long desired to do, what else can I say but that they were inspired words, which were heard by the courtiers and by us who had entered with them?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This was the beginning and first establishment of the Emperor’s kindly feeling towards us; the impression produced by this reception put an end to the greater part of the persecution which assailed us like a river.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Another incident is not of less importance than those I have mentioned.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The wicked were victorious, and the decree for his banishment was signed, to the full satisfaction of those who furthered it.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The night had come, the chariot was ready, our haters were exultant, the pious in despair, we surrounded the zealous traveller, to whose honourable disgrace nothing was wanting.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It was undone by God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For He Who smote the first-born of Egypt, for its harshness towards Israel, also struck the son of the Emperor with disease.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How great was the speed!

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There was the sentence of banishment, here the decree of sickness: the hand of the wicked scribe was restrained, and the saint was preserved, and the man of piety presented to us, by the fever which brought to reason the arrogance of the Emperor.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What could be more just or more speedy than this?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This was the series of events: the Emperor’s child was sick and in bodily pain.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The father was pained for it, for what can the father do?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: On all sides he sought for aid in his distress, he summoned the best physicians, he betook himself to intercessions with the greatest fervour, and flung himself upon the ground.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Affliction humbles even emperors, and no wonder, for the like sufferings of David in the case of his child are recorded for us.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But as no cure for the evil could anywhere be found, he applied to the faith of Basil, not personally summoning him, in shame for his recent ill treatment, but entrusting the mission to others of his nearest and dearest friends.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: On his arrival, without the delay or reluctance which any one else might have shown, at once the disease relaxed, and the father cherished better hopes; and had he not blended salt water with the fresh, by trusting to the heterodox at the same time that he summoned Basil, the child would have recovered his health and been preserved for his father’s arms.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This indeed was the conviction of those who were present at the time, and shared in the distress.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The same mischance is said to have befallen the prefect.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He also was obliged by sickness to bow beneath the hands of the Saint, and, in reality, to men of sense a visitation brings instruction, and affliction is often better than prosperity.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He fell sick, was in tears, and in pain, he sent for Basil, and entreated him, crying out, “I own that you were in the right; only save me!”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: His request was granted, as he himself acknowledged, and convinced many who had known nothing of it; for he never ceased to wonder at and describe the powers of the prelate.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such was his conduct in these cases, such its result.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Did he then treat others in a different way, and engage in petty disputes about trifles, or fail to rise to the heights of philosophy in a course of action which merits no praise and is best passed over in silence?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: By no means.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He who once stirred up the wicked Hadad against Israel, stirred up against him the prefect of the province of Pontus; nominally, from annoyance connected with some poor creature of a woman, but in reality as a part of the struggle of impiety against the truth.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I pass by all his other insults against Basil, or, for it is the same thing, against God; for it is against Him and on His behalf that the contest was waged.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: One instance of it, however, which brought special disgrace upon the assailant, and exalted his adversary, if philosophy and eminence for it be a great and lofty thing, I will describe at length.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The assessor of a judge was attempting to force into a distasteful marriage a lady of high birth whose husband was but recently dead.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: At a loss to escape from this high-handed treatment, she resorted to a device no less prudent than daring.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: She fled to the holy table, and placed herself under the protection of God against outrage.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What, in the Name of the Trinity Itself, if I may introduce into my panegyric somewhat of the forensic style, ought to have been done, I do not say, by the great Basil, who laid down the law for us all in such matters, but by any one who, though far inferior to him, was a priest?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Ought he not to have allowed her claim, to have taken charge of, and cared for, her; to have raised his hand in defence of the kindness of God and the law which gives honour to the altar?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Ought he not to have been willing to do and suffer anything, rather than take part in any inhuman design against her, and outrage at once the holy table, and the faith in which she had taken sanctuary?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: No! said the baffled judge, all ought to yield to my authority, and Christians should betray their own laws.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The suppliant whom he demanded, was at all hazards retained.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Accordingly, in his rage, he at last sent some of the magistrates to search the saint’s bedchamber, with the purpose of dishonouring him, rather than from any necessity.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Search the house of a man so free from passion, whom the angels revere, at whom women do not venture even to look?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And, not content with this, he summoned him, and put him on his defence; and that, in no gentle or kindly manner, but as if he were a convict.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Upon Basil’s appearance, standing, like my Jesus, before the judgment seat of Pilate, he presided at the trial, full of wrath and pride.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Yet the thunderbolts did not fall, and the sword of God still glittered, and waited, while His bow, though bent, was restrained.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such indeed is the custom of God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Consider another struggle between our champion and his persecutor.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: His ragged pallium having been ordered to be torn away, “I will also, if you wish it, strip off my coat,” said he.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: His fleshless form was threatened with blows, and he offered to submit to be torn with combs, and he said, “By such laceration you will cure my liver, which, as you see, is wearing me away.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such was their argument.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But when the city perceived the outrage and the common danger of all—for each one considered this insolence a danger to himself, it became all on fire with rage; and, like a hive roused by smoke, one after another was stirred and arose, every race and every age, but especially the men from the small-arms factory and from the imperial weaving-sheds.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For men at work in these trades are specially hot-tempered and daring, because of the liberty allowed them.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Each man was armed with the tool he was using, or with whatever else came to hand at the moment.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Torch in hand, amid showers of stones, with cudgel’s ready, all ran and shouted together in their united zeal.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Anger makes a terrible soldier or general.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Nor were the women weaponless, when roused by such an occasion.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Their pins were their spears, and no longer remaining women, they were by the strength of their eagerness endowed with masculine courage.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It is a short story.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They thought that they would share among themselves the piety of destroying him, and held him to be most pious who first laid hands on one who had dared such deeds.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What then was the conduct of this haughty and daring judge?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He begged for mercy in a pitiable state of distress, cringing before them to an unparalleled extent, until the arrival of the martyr without bloodshed, who had won his crown without blows, and now restrained the people by the force of his personal influence, and delivered the man who had insulted him and now sought his protection.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This was the doing of the God of Saints, Who worketh and changeth all things for the best, who resisteth the proud, but giveth grace to the humble.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And why should not He, Who divided the sea and stayed the river, and ruled the elements, and by stretching out set up a trophy, to save His exiled people, why should not He have also rescued this man from his perils?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: This was the end and fortunate close, in the Providence of God, of the war with the world, a close worthy of his faith.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But here at once is the beginning of the war with the Bishops, and their allies, which involved great disgrace, and still greater injury to their subjects.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For who could persuade others to be temperate, when such was the conduct of their prelates?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For a long time they had been unkindly disposed towards him, on three grounds.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They neither agreed with him in the matter of the faith, except in so far as they were absolutely obliged to yield to the majority of the faithful.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Nor had they altogether laid aside the grudge they owed him for his election.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And, what was most grievous of all to them, though they would have been most ashamed to own it—he so far outshone them in reputation.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There was also a further cause of dissension which stirred up again the others.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: When our country had been divided into two provinces and metropolitical sees, and a great part of the former was being added to the new one, this again roused their factious spirit.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The one thought it right that the ecclesiastical boundaries should be settled by the civil ones: and therefore claimed those newly added, as belonging to him, and severed from their former metropolitan.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The other clung to the ancient custom, and to the division which had come down from our fathers.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Many painful results either actually followed, or were struggling in the womb of the future.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Synods were wrongfully gathered by the new metropolitan, and revenues seized upon.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Some of the presbyters of the churches refused obedience, others were won over.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In consequence the affairs of the churches fell into a sad state of dissension and division.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Novelty indeed has a certain charm for men, and they readily turn events to their own advantage, and it is easier to overthrow something which is already established, than to restore it when overthrown.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What however enraged him most was, that the revenues of the Taurus, which passed along before his eyes, accrued to his rival, as also the offerings at Saint Orestes’, of which he was greatly desirous to reap the fruits.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He even went so far as, on one occasion when Basil was riding along his own road, to seize his mules by the bridle and bar the passage with a robber band.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And with how specious a pretext, the care of his spiritual children and of the souls entrusted to him, and the defence of the faith—pretexts which veiled that most common vice, insatiable avarice—and further, the wrongfulness of paying dues to heretics, a heretic being any one who had displeased him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The holy man of God however, metropolitan as he was of the true Jerusalem above, was neither carried away with the failure of those who fell, nor allowed himself to overlook this conduct, nor did he desire any inadequate remedy for the evil.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Let us see how great and wonderful it was, or, I would say, how worthy of his soul.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He made of the dissension a cause of increase to the Church, and the disaster, under his most able management, resulted in the multiplication of the Bishops of the country.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: From this ensued three most desirable consequences; a greater care for souls, the management by each city of its own affairs, and the cessation of the war in this quarter.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I am afraid that I myself was treated as an appendage to this scheme.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: By no other term can I readily describe the position.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Greatly as I admire his whole conduct, to an extent indeed beyond my powers of expression, of this single particular I find it impossible to approve, for I will acknowledge my feelings in regard to it, though these are from other sources not unknown to most of you.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I mean the change and faithlessness of his treatment of myself, a cause of pain which even time has not obliterated.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For this is the source of all the inconsistency and tangle of my life; it has robbed me of the practice, or at least the reputation, of philosophy; of small moment though the latter be.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The defence, which you will perhaps allow me to make for him, is this; his ideas were superhuman, and having, before his death, become superior to worldly influences, his only interests were those of the Spirit: while his regard for friendship was in no wise lessened by his readiness then, and then only, to disregard its claims, when they were in conflict with his paramount duty to God, and when the end he had in view was of greater importance than the interests he was compelled to set aside.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I am afraid that, in avoiding the imputation of indifference at the hands of those who desire to know all that can be said about him, I shall incur a charge of prolixity from those whose ideal is the golden mean.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For the latter Basil himself had the greatest respect, being specially devoted to the adage “In all things the mean is the best,” and acting upon it throughout his life.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Nevertheless, disregarding alike those who desire undue conciseness or excessive prolixity, I proceed thus with my speech.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Different men attain success in different ways, some applying themselves to one alone of the many forms of excellence, but no one, of those hitherto known to me, arriving at the highest eminence in all respects; he being in my opinion the best, who has won his laurels on the widest field, or gained the highest possible renown in some single particular.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such however was the height of Basil’s fame, that he became the pride of human kind.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Let us consider the matter thus.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Is any one devoted to poverty and a life devoid of property, and free from superfluity?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What did he possess besides his body, and the necessary coverings of the flesh?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: His wealth was the having nothing, and he thought the cross, with which he lived, more precious than great riches.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For no one, however much he may wish, can obtain possession of all things, but any one can learn to despise, and so prove himself superior to, all things.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such being his mind, and such his life, he had no need of an altar and of vainglory, nor of such a public announcement as “Crates sets Crates the Theban free.”

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For his aim was ever to be, not to seem, most excellent.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Nor did he dwell in a tub, and in the midst of the market-place, and so by luxuriating in publicity turn his poverty into riches: but was poor and unkempt, yet without ostentation: and taking cheerfully the casting overboard of all that he ever had, sailed lightly across the sea of life.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: A wondrous thing is temperance, and fewness of wants, and freedom from the dominion of pleasures, and from the bondage of that cruel and degrading mistress, the belly.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who was so independent of food, and, without exaggeration, more free from the flesh?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For he flung away all satiety and surfeit to creatures destitute of reason, whose life is slavish and debasing.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He paid little attention to such things as, next to the appetite, are of equal rank, but, as far as possible, lived on the merest necessaries, his only luxury being to prove himself not luxurious, and not, in consequence, to have greater needs: but he looked to the lilies and the birds, whose beauty is artless, and their food casual, according to the important advice of my Christ, who made Himself poor in the flesh for our sakes, that we might enjoy the riches of His Godhead.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Hence came his single coat and well worn cloak, and his bed on the bare ground, his vigils, his unwashedness (such were his decorations) and his most sweet food and relish, bread, and salt, his new dainty, and the sober and plentiful drink, with which fountains supply those who are free from trouble.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The result, or the accompaniment, of these things were the attendance on the sick and practice of medicine, our common intellectual pursuit.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For, though inferior to him in all other respects, I must needs be his equal in distress.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: A great thing is virginity, and celibacy, and being ranked with the angels, and with the single nature; for I shrink from calling it Christ’s, Who, though He willed to be born for our sakes who are born, by being born of a Virgin, enacted the law of virginity, to lead us away from this life, and cut short the power of the world, or rather, to transmit one world to another, the present to the future.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who then paid more honour to virginity, or had more control of the flesh, not only by his personal example, but in those under his care?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Whose are the convents, and the written regulations, by which he subdued every sense, and regulated every member, and won to the real practice of virginity, turning inward the view of beauty, from the visible to the invisible; and by wasting away the external, and withdrawing fuel from the flame, and revealing the secrets of the heart to God, Who is the only bridegroom of pure souls, and takes in with himself the watchful souls, if they go to meet him with lamps burning and a plentiful supply of oil?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Moreover he reconciled most excellently and united the solitary and the community life.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: These had been in many respects at variance and dissension, while neither of them was in absolute and unalloyed possession of good or evil: the one being more calm and settled, tending to union with God, yet not free from pride, inasmuch as its virtue lies beyond the means of testing or comparison; the other, which is of more practical service, being not free from the tendency to turbulence.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He founded cells for ascetics and hermits, but at no great distance from his cenobitic communities, and, instead of distinguishing and separating the one from the other, as if by some intervening wall, he brought them together and united them, in order that the contemplative spirit might not be cut off from society, nor the active life be uninfluenced by the contemplative, but that, like sea and land, by an interchange of their several gifts, they might unite in promoting the one object, the glory of God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: A noble thing is philanthropy, and the support of the poor, and the assistance of human weakness.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Go forth a little way from the city, and behold the new city, the storehouse of piety, the common treasury of the wealthy, in which the superfluities of their wealth, aye, and even their necessaries, are stored, in consequence of his exhortations, freed from the power of the moth, no longer gladdening the eyes of the thief, and escaping both the emulation of envy, and the corruption of time: where disease is regarded in a religious light, and disaster is thought a blessing, and sympathy is put to the test.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Why should I compare with this work Thebes of the seen portals, and the Egyptian Thebes, and the walls of Babylon, and the Carian tomb of Mausolus, and the Pyramids, and the bronze without weight of the Colossus, or the size and beauty of shrines that are no more, and all the other objects of men’s wonder, and historic record, from which their founders gained no advantage, except a slight meed of fame.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: My subject is the most wonderful of all, the short road to salvation, the easiest ascent to heaven.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There is no longer before our eyes that terrible and piteous spectacle of men who are living corpses, the greater part of whose limbs have mortified, driven away from their cities and homes and public places and fountains, aye, and from their own dearest ones, recognizable by their names rather than by their features: they are no longer brought before us at our gatherings and meetings, in our common intercourse and union, no longer the objects of hatred, instead of pity on account of their disease; composers of piteous songs, if any of them have their voice still left to them.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Why should I try to express in tragic style all our experiences, when no language can be adequate to their hard lot?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He however it was, who took the lead in pressing upon those who were men, that they ought not to despise their fellowmen, nor to dishonour Christ, the one Head of all, by their inhuman treatment of them; but to use the misfortunes of others as an opportunity of firmly establishing their own lot, and to lend to God that mercy of which they stand in need at His hands.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He did not therefore disdain to honour with his lips this disease, noble and of noble ancestry and brilliant reputation though he was, but saluted them as brethren, not, as some might suppose, from vainglory, (for who was so far removed from this feeling?) but taking the lead in approaching to tend them, as a consequence of his philosophy, and so giving not only a speaking, but also a silent, instruction.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The effect produced is to be seen not only in the city, but in the country and beyond, and even the leaders of society have vied with one another in their philanthropy and magnanimity towards them.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Others have had their cooks, and splendid tables, and the devices and dainties of confectioners, and exquisite carriages, and soft, flowing robes; Basil’s care was for the sick, and the relief of their wounds, and the imitation of Christ, by cleansing leprosy, not by a word, but in deed.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: As to all this, what will be said by those who charge him with pride and haughtiness?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Severe critics they are of such conduct, applying to him, whose life was a standard, those who were not standards at all.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Is it possible that he who kissed the lepers, and humiliated himself to such a degree, could treat haughtily those who were in health: and, while wasting his flesh by abstinence, puff out his soul with empty arrogance?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Is it possible to condemn the Pharisee, and expound the debasing effect of haughtiness, to know Christ, Who condescended to the form of a slave, and ate with publicans, and washed the disciples’ feet, and did not disdain the cross, in order to nail my sin to it: and, more incredible still, to see God crucified, aye, along with robbers also, and derided by the passers by, impassible, and beyond the reach of suffering as He is; and yet, as his slanderers imagine, soar himself above the clouds, and think that nothing can be on an equality with him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Nay, what they term pride is, I fancy, the firmness and steadfastness and stability of his character.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such persons would readily, it seems to me, call bravery rashness, and the circumspect a coward, and the temperate misanthropic, and the just illiberal.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For indeed this philosophic axiom is excellent, which says that the vices are settled close to the virtues, and are, in some sense, their next-door neighbours: and it is most easy, for those whose training in such subjects has been defective, to mistake a man for what he is not.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For who honoured virtue and castigated vice more than he, or showed himself more kind to the upright, more severe to the wrong doers?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: His very smile often amounted to praise, his silence to rebuke, racking the evil in the secret conscience.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if a man have not been a chatterer, and jester, and gossip, nor a general favourite, because of having pleased others by becoming all things to all men, what of that?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Is he not in the eyes of sensible men worthy of praise rather than of blame?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Unless it is a fault in the lion that he is terrible and royal, and does not look like an ape, and that his spring is noble, and is valued for its wonderfulness: while stage-players ought to win our admiration for their pleasant and philanthropic characters, because they please the vulgar, and raise a laugh by their sounding slaps in the face.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And if this indeed be our object, who was so pleasant when you met him, as I know, who have had the longest experience?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who was more kindly in his stories, more refined in his wit, more tender in his rebukes?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: His reproofs gave rise to no arrogance, his relaxation to no dissipation, but avoiding excess in either, he made use of both in reason and season, according to the rules of Solomon, who assigns to every business a season.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But what are these to his renown for eloquence, and his powers of instruction, which have won the favour of the ends of the world?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: As yet we have been compassing the foot of the mountain, to the neglect of its summit, as yet we have been crossing a strait, paying no heed to the mighty and deep ocean.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For I think that if any one ever has become, or can become, a trumpet, in his far sounding resonance, or a voice of God, embracing the universe, or an earthquake of the world, by some unheard of miracle, it is his voice and intellect which deserve these titles, for surpassing and excelling all men as much as we surpass the irrational creatures.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who, more than he, cleansed himself by the Spirit, and made himself worthy to set forth divine things?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Who was more enlightened by the light of knowledge, and had a closer insight into the depths of the Spirit, and by the aid of God beheld the things of God?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Whose language could better express intellectual truth, without, as most men do, limping on one foot, by either failing to express his ideas, or allowing his eloquence to outstrip his reasoning powers?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In both respects he won a like distinction, and showed himself to be his own equal, and absolutely perfect.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: To search all things, yea, the deep things of God is, according to the testimony of S.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Paul, the office of the Spirit, not because He is ignorant of them, but because He takes delight in their contemplation.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Now all the things of the Spirit Basil had fully investigated, and hence he drew his instructions for every kind of character, his lessons in the sublime, and his exhortations to quit things present, and adapt ourselves to things to come.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The sun is extolled by David for its beauty, its greatness, its swift course, and its power, splendid as a bridegroom, majestic as a giant; while, from the extent of its circuit, it has such power that it equally sheds its light from one end of heaven to the other, and the heat thereof is in no wise lessened by distance.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Basil’s beauty was virtue, his greatness theology, his course the perpetual motion reaching even to God by its ascents, and his power the sowing and distribution of the Word.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: So that I will not hesitate to say even this, his utterance went out into all lands, and the power of his words to the ends of the world: as S.

verses.text: Paul says of the Apostles, borrowing the words from David.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What other charm is there in any gathering to-day?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What pleasure in banquets, in the courts, in the churches?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What delight in those in authority, and those beneath them?

verses.text: What in the hermits, or the cenobites?
